stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: OKO Group (Doronin) advancing 53-story, 117-unit condo tower in Miami Edgewater; 13th Floor proposing 75-story, 321-unit Nobu tower in Brickell.
Both projects cleared Miami UDRB design review; final city approval required before construction.
Ultra-luxury condo market remains active in Miami despite broader market headwinds; little supply constraint at high end.
stories.summary: Two luxury condo towers—OKO Lilli (117 units, Edgewater) and 619 Brickell by Nobu (321 units, Brickell)—advanced past Miami's Urban Development Review Board. Designed by top architecture firms (Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill, ODP), both projects feature high-end finishes and branded amenities. UDRB approval is advisory; city planning director makes final decision. OKO Lilli includes 244 parking spaces and ground-floor retail; Nobu tower replaces parking and school lot adjacent to historic church.
stories.impact: Both are condo, not multifamily rental, so limited direct relevance to apartment development. However, they signal continued capital confidence in Miami luxury residential despite market headwinds. For rental developers, the absorption of these ultra-luxury units may reduce condo conversion risk but does not materially shift rental demand curves in Miami—which remains driven by workforce migration and affordability constraints, not luxury supply.

stories.title: CRE Comes Together at MADE Bush Terminal for Cocktails and an Exciting New Development
stories.excerpt: On a humid and rainy July night in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, people from throughout the commercial real estate industry escaped the elements at the new mixed-use complex MADE Bush Terminal, which hosted Commercial Observer’s annual summer networking mixer. Amid a series of massive square wooden columns conveying the complex’s industrial feel, attendees networked, enjoyed drinks and small bites, and heard from some of MADE’s pioneering tenants on the benefits of taking space at the complex, which, in addition to sites like a brewery and a new 1,000-capacity music venue, will mostly host companies with light industrial uses. Bridget Chansakul, assistant vice president at the New York City Economic Development Corporation (NYCEDC), handles leasing for the development, and was excited for people to see the goings-on at Building A, where the event took place. While several tenants are already in place in that building, as well as in the project’s Building B, the full development and unveiling of MADE Bush Terminal, which will eventually be a five-building complex, will occur slowly over the next few years. “We’re giving a sneak peek tonight, but it’s just the beginning of what’s happening here in Sunset Park,” said Chansakul. “We’re getting ready to announce a new brewery coming to the site and we’re going to be unveiling a new building, so it’s just the beginning of an exciting and much larger project.” After brief introductions from Brian Pascus, a finance reporter at Commercial Observer, and Jennifer Brown, senior vice president for the Sunset Park district at NYCEDC, attendees were treated to a brief discussion for a deeper introduction to MADE Bush Terminal. Waverly Neer, vice president of asset management at NYCEDC, held a short panel discussion with two of the building’s earliest tenants: lighting manufacturer Stickbulb and design studio Pelle. Pelle, run by husband and wife architect team Jean and Oliver Pelle, moved into Building
bullets: MADE Bush Terminal (Sunset Park, Brooklyn) hosted mixed-use networking event; hosts brewery, 1,000-capacity music venue, light industrial tenants.
NYCEDC assists leasing; Building A already occupied by multiple tenants.
Industrial-mixed-use hybrid model gaining traction in post-industrial Brooklyn submarkets.
stories.summary: Commercial Observer held summer networking event at MADE Bush Terminal, a new mixed-use development in Sunset Park, Brooklyn featuring light industrial, food-and-beverage, and live-event components. NYCEDC's Bridget Chansakul handles leasing. Building A is already leased to early tenants. The event highlighted the complex's role as a hub for creative and logistics operators seeking flexible industrial space in Brooklyn.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. MADE Bush Terminal is industrial-retail-hospitality mixed-use; does not add rental apartment supply or signal demand shifts. However, the continued viability of mixed-use industrial redevelopment in Sunset Park suggests sustained demand for flexible creative/logistics space, which may compete with residential zoning conversion opportunities on similar sites in the neighborhood.

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to deny Project Tango zoning for 1M SF data center, 2.3M SF warehouses on ~200-acre parcel.
PBA Holdings owns ~130 acres; 12-hour hearing with 80+ speakers opposed; motion denied without prejudice, allowing resubmission.
Data center demand rejected; industrial/warehouse already approved; community opposition halted tech infrastructure.
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners rejected a zoning application for Project Tango (Central Park Commerce Center), which would have permitted a 1 million-square-foot data center plus 2.3 million square feet of warehouses on a ~200-acre parcel in unincorporated county. PBA Holdings, a joint venture of four entities, filed the application. Hundreds of community members and over 80 public speakers opposed the project, citing infrastructure and community impacts. The developer had already secured approval for 2 million square feet of warehousing but can pursue data centers via separate permitting.
stories.impact: Data center rejection signals strong community resistance to dense tech infrastructure in exurban South Florida, which may slow industrial/logistics land value appreciation in Palm Beach County's peripheral corridors. For multifamily, this reduces the likelihood of job anchors and population magnets from data-center payroll in the area. However, the earlier warehouse approval remains valid, so light logistics and fulfillment tenants may still drive some inbound worker migration and rental demand in West Palm Beach and surrounding submarkets—albeit at lower velocity than if a major tech HQ or data campus had been greenlit.

stories.title: Stetson’s Law School, Bromley Partnering on Tampa Mixed-Use Venture
stories.excerpt: Stetson University plans to modernize the Stetson University College of Law’s Tampa Law Center and create a mixed-use space in a currently underutilized area with the help of Bromley Companies. Bromley hopes to maintain Stetson Law’s presence but add hotel rooms, apartments, restaurants and more. On the 6.5-acre site, Bromley envisions 664 multifamily units, 440,000 square feet of office space, 37,450 square feet of retail space and 300 hotel keys The developer is aiming for a late 2027 groundbreaking. The redevelopment will occur in phases, with no disruption to Stetson’s classes or operation on the property. The university filed a rezoning application to make way for the project, which will require approval from Tampa City Council. The University will retain ownership of the Stetson Law Center and have permanent access to use of the hotel and parking garage. The post Stetson’s Law School, Bromley Partnering on Tampa Mixed-Use Venture appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Stetson University College of Law partnering with Bromley Companies on 6.5-acre Tampa mixed-use redevelopment: 664 multifamily units, 440K SF office, 37.5K SF retail, 300 hotel keys.
Late 2027 groundbreaking target; phased development; university retains law center ownership and hotel/parking access.
Rezoning application filed with Tampa City Council; no disruption to ongoing law classes.
stories.summary: Stetson University and Bromley Companies plan a phased mixed-use redevelopment of the Stetson Law Tampa Law Center and surrounding 6.5-acre site. The project includes 664 apartment units, 440,000 square feet of office, 37,450 square feet of retail, and 300 hotel rooms. Groundbreaking targeted for late 2027. The university retains ownership of the law center and permanent access to hotel and parking. A rezoning application was filed with Tampa City Council.
stories.impact: 664 units in Tampa market signals institutional anchor-driven multifamily development gaining traction in tier-2 Sun Belt markets. Bromley's presence and scale (664 units + mixed-use) indicates strong developer confidence in Tampa rental fundamentals driven by education anchor tenancy, professional workforce demand (law school), and hospitality integration. This project de-risks site assembly in underutilized central urban locations and may attract additional capital to similar university-adjacent mixed-use opportunities in Tampa, lifting rents and cap rates in the education corridor.

stories.title: Apartment Developer Pursuing Phillips Place for Multifamily Community
stories.excerpt: Mid-America Apartment Communities is seeking a zoning change to build a 275-unit apartment complex and 15,000 square feet of commercial space at Phillips Place in SouthPark. The Charlotte Business Journal reports the apartment community would be built where the former Regal Cinemas used to stand. The movie theater was demolished in 2022. Phillips Place, a 134,000-square-foot mixed-use center, was built by Lincoln Property Co. and opened in 1997. Phillips Place is now owned by Simon Property Group Inc. The national retail developer acquired the property in November 2025 from Lincoln and The Village Collection for $144.8 million. MAA owns and operates more than 300 properties in 16 states and Washington, D.C. The firm entered the Charlotte market in 2010. Its local portfolio now includes more than 20 properties across the region. The post Apartment Developer Pursuing Phillips Place for Multifamily Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) seeking zoning change for 275-unit apartment + 15K SF retail at Phillips Place SouthPark, Charlotte.
Site of former Regal Cinemas (demolished 2022); Simon Property Group acquired Phillips Place in Nov 2025 for $144.8M.
MAA operates 300+ properties across 16 states; Charlotte portfolio now 20+ properties since 2010 entry.
stories.summary: Mid-America Apartment Communities is pursuing a zoning change to develop a 275-unit apartment complex and 15,000 square feet of retail at Phillips Place in SouthPark, Charlotte, on the site of the former Regal Cinemas demolished in 2022. Simon Property Group acquired the 134,000-square-foot mixed-use center in November 2025 from Lincoln and The Village Collection for $144.8 million. MAA has operated 300+ properties in 16 states and entered Charlotte in 2010, now operating 20+ properties in the region.
stories.impact: SPG's recent $144.8M acquisition of Phillips Place suggests REITs are actively repositioning underperforming retail centers into mixed-use (retail + residential) through partnership with apartment operators. This signals capital re-direction from pure retail to hybrid models in tier-1 suburbs. For multifamily developers, the MAA zoning push indicates strong Charlotte rental demand and willingness from major operators to convert Class-B retail anchors to residential. Success on this 275-unit conversion could unlock similar opportunities across Charlotte's aging retail base, accelerating supply and potentially moderating rent growth in SouthPark and similar submarkets.

stories.title: S2 Capital Offloads 229-Unit Plano Rental Community
stories.excerpt: Newmark arranged the sale and financing of Hathaway at Willow Bend, a 229-unit multifamily community located at 2525 Preston Road in Plano, Texas. Newmark’s Richard Furr, Brian O’Boyle, Jr., Brian Murphy and Jack Forman represented the seller, S2 Capital Acquisitions, in the transaction. Brian Kochan and Michael Caglianone arranged acquisition financing on behalf of the buyer, Granite Towers Equity Group.  Located along Preston Road in West Plano, Hathaway at Willow Bend is a garden-style community offering one-, two- and three-bedroom residences, including a variety of townhome floor plans. Amenities include two swimming pools, an outdoor kitchen, bark park, natural creek setting and fitness center. Apartment interiors include gourmet kitchens and wood-burning fireplaces.  According to Newmark Research, the Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily market continues to benefit from strong demographic and employment fundamentals. Dallas-Fort Worth led the nation in employment growth from 2019 through 2026, adding more than 604,400 jobs, a 16.3% increase. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post S2 Capital Offloads 229-Unit Plano Rental Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: S2 Capital Acquisitions sold Hathaway at Willow Bend, 229-unit garden-style community in West Plano, Texas, to Granite Towers Equity Group.
DFW multifamily market added 604.4K jobs (16.3% growth) 2019–2026, strongest U.S. employment growth.
Newmark arranged sale and acquisition financing; continued investor appetite for Plano rental assets.
stories.summary: S2 Capital Acquisitions sold Hathaway at Willow Bend, a 229-unit garden-style multifamily community at 2525 Preston Road in West Plano, Texas, to Granite Towers Equity Group. Newmark arranged the sale and acquisition financing. The Dallas-Fort Worth market added more than 604,400 jobs from 2019 through 2026, a 16.3% increase—leading the nation in employment growth. The property offers one-, two-, and three-bedroom residences, including townhomes, plus amenities (pools, fitness, bark park).
stories.impact: DFW's outsized employment growth (604K jobs, #1 U.S. 2019–2026) continues to drive multifamily investor demand and price appreciation. The sale of a stabilized 229-unit asset signals strong secondary buyer demand for Class-B multifamily in West Plano suburbs. Granite Towers' acquisition and Newmark's financing arrangement indicate capital remains readily available for DFW multifamily despite broader market tightening. For developers, DFW's sustained job growth and tight cap rates imply limited new-supply opportunity at market rates; focus should shift to workforce/affordable or premium urban infill to differentiate and capture pricing power.

stories.title: NYC Multifamily Sales Reflect “Two Very Different Markets” in Q2
stories.excerpt: New York City multifamily sales totaled $2.46 billion across 298 transactions in the second quarter of 2026, representing a 25% year-over-year increase in dollar volume but 4% decline in transaction volume compared to Q2 2025, Ariel Property Advisors reported. The number of properties traded was down 11% Y-O-Y to 367. “What we are seeing in the first half of 2026 is a tale of two very different markets, both presenting unique opportunities,” said Shimon Shkury, president and founder of Ariel. “On one side, Manhattan’s free-market sector is showing exceptional strength, rents are up 10% year-over-year, and high-conviction buyers are writing significantly larger checks for supply-constrained assets. “On the other side, rent-stabilized assets are undergoing a massive, painful valuation reset amidst rising expenses and refinancings at double their original rate. Longtime rent-stabilized owners are exiting, and a new wave of buyers is stepping in to acquire these properties at steep discounts. Meanwhile, affordable housing remains a resilient safe haven, drawing deep institutional demand due to its stable, government-backed income.” Manhattan below 96th Street fared best in Q2 with a 315% increase Y-O-Y in dollar volume and 21% increase in transaction volume. Conversely, Brooklyn, the Bronx and Queens all saw double-digit declines in dollar volume. The post NYC Multifamily Sales Reflect “Two Very Different Markets” in Q2 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NYC Q2 2026 multifamily sales: $2.46B across 298 transactions (+25% Y-O-Y dollar volume, -4% Y-O-Y transaction count); 367 properties traded (-11% Y-O-Y).
Manhattan below 96th St. (+315% Y-O-Y dollar volume, +21% transaction count); rents +10% Y-O-Y; free-market strength vs. rent-stabilized distress.
Brooklyn, Bronx, Queens all saw double-digit dollar-volume declines; rent-stabilized owners exiting; affordable housing draws institutional capital.
stories.summary: NYC multifamily sales totaled $2.46 billion across 298 transactions in Q2 2026, up 25% year-over-year in dollar volume but down 4% in transaction count, with 367 properties traded (down 11% Y-O-Y). Manhattan below 96th Street led with a 315% increase in dollar volume and 21% increase in transaction volume. Manhattan free-market rents are up 10% Y-O-Y, with high-conviction buyers acquiring supply-constrained assets at steep premiums. Conversely, rent-stabilized assets face a valuation reset amid rising expenses and refinancings at double historical rates; longtime owners are exiting. Brooklyn, the Bronx, and Queens all posted double-digit dollar-volume declines. Affordable housing remains resilient, drawing deep institutional demand.
stories.impact: NYC multifamily market has bifurcated sharply: free-market Manhattan strength (+315% dollar volume, +10% rent growth) reflects flight-to-quality and supply constraint, while rent-stabilized and outer-borough assets face forced exits and distress sales. For developers, this signals: (1) Manhattan free-market development commands investor premiums despite rates/cap spreads; (2) rent-stabilized portfolio acquisitions at discounts may offer value for operators with 10+ year hold horizons; (3) affordable housing (government-backed, stable cash flow) remains defensive and attracts institutional capital, justifying new construction in all boroughs if subsidy/LIHTC available. Outer-borough weakness suggests softer rents and selective development—prioritize only highest-growth/employment corridors (Long Island City, Astoria, Downtown Brooklyn).

stories.title: Dallas Developer Duo Opens 168-Unit Mixed-Income Community
stories.excerpt: Bonner Carrington and Sycamore Development opened Cypress Creek Montfort Drive, a mixed-income community of 168 apartment homes in North Dallas. Located at 14119 Montfort Drive, Cypress Creek Montfort Drive features 116 apartment homes that are income-restricted for residents earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income (AMI), with 52 apartment homes offered at market rate. One-, two-, and three-bedroom apartment homes are available with private balconies. Community amenities include a fitness center, an outdoor grilling station by the pool, a business center with private work pods, and a pet spa. Financing for the community was made possible through the collaboration of multiple public and private partners, including: ·     Federal 9% Low-Income Housing Tax Credit equity syndicated by Hunt Capital Partners, awarded by the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs  ·     Construction and permanent financing from East West Bank  ·     A soft loan from the City of Dallas  The community was built by Moss Construction, with Arrive Architecture Group serving as architect. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Dallas Developer Duo Opens 168-Unit Mixed-Income Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Bonner Carrington and Sycamore Development opened Cypress Creek Montfort Drive, 168-unit mixed-income community in North Dallas: 116 units income-restricted (30–80% AMI), 52 market-rate.
Federal 9% LIHTC equity (Hunt Capital Partners), construction/permanent financing from East West Bank, City of Dallas soft loan.
One-, two-, three-bedroom homes; fitness, pool, business center, pet spa; mixed-income model signals strong public-private collaboration.
stories.summary: Bonner Carrington and Sycamore Development opened Cypress Creek Montfort Drive, a 168-unit mixed-income community in North Dallas (14119 Montfort Drive). The project comprises 116 income-restricted apartments (30–80% AMI) and 52 market-rate units. Financing included federal 9% LIHTC equity syndicated by Hunt Capital Partners (via TDHCA), construction and permanent financing from East West Bank, and a soft loan from the City of Dallas. Amenities include fitness, pool, business center, and pet spa. Moss Construction was the general contractor; Arrive Architecture Group the designer.
stories.impact: Mixed-income LIHTC delivery in Dallas signals strong tax-credit market conditions and public financing availability, which typically precedes broader multifamily expansion. Public-private capital stack (federal LIHTC, bank financing, city soft loan) indicates Dallas municipalities are actively deploying incentives to support affordable/workforce housing amid high market-rate rents. For developers, success in LIHTC-financed mixed-income projects de-risks affordable components and unlocks institutional investor pools; expect similar projects to cluster in North Dallas and suburbs where land costs and labor remain feasible for 9% credit returns. This signals emerging affordability pressure in Dallas rental market warranting subsidized product supply.

stories.title: Logistics Firm Highline Commerce Grows to 60K SF at Industry City, Thanks to Robots
stories.excerpt: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce is expanding its industrial footprint at Brooklyn’s Industry City for the second time in four years, Commercial Observer has learned. Highline’s lease renewal at 67 34th Street in Brooklyn’s Sunset Park neighborhood tacked on another 18,000 square feet to its warehouse space. The five-year deal rounds out the company’s total footprint at the mixed-use commercial campus to 60,000 square feet. The Brooklyn-based logistics company specializes in fulfilling orders for early-stage e-commerce brands, according to its website. Highline credits its growth, in part, to neighborly robots. The company has teamed up with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics to install “humanoid robots” that are fulfilling up to 30 percent of Highline’s clients’ orders, according to an Industry City spokesperson. The 60,000-square-foot flexible industrial space will remain largely dedicated to fulfillment operations, along with some office space and social rooms. Highline Commerce negotiated the deal directly with Industry City’s Jeff Fein and Brad Blum, who represent the landlord in-house. Asking rent was $28 per square foot. Highline arrived at Industry City from Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood in 2022. During its initial four-year lease, the company’s warehouse expanded from 27,000 square feet to 42,000 square feet. Highline plans to remain at Industry City as long as it can keep growing. “New York is in the middle of an e-commerce golden age, and brands are launching faster than the logistics world can support them,” Richard Hurley, founder and CEO of Highline, said in a statement. “Serving them doesn’t just take a village, it takes a place like Industry City, where on-site manufacturing, printing, fulfillment and the robots that run it all operate within feet of one another.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Highline Commerce, third-party logistics firm, expanded at Brooklyn's Industry City from 42K to 60K SF (18K SF addition); five-year lease renewal.
Tenant uses humanoid robots (Ultra Robotics partner) to fulfill 30% of client orders; heavy robotics/automation deployment in urban logistics.
Second expansion in four years signals robust e-commerce fulfillment demand and tech-enabled industrial viability in Sunset Park.
stories.summary: Highline Commerce, a Brooklyn-based third-party logistics provider specializing in early-stage e-commerce fulfillment, renewed and expanded its lease at Industry City in Sunset Park from 42,000 square feet to 60,000 square feet (18,000 SF addition) on a five-year deal. The company has now more than doubled its footprint at the mixed-use industrial campus in four years. Highline has partnered with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics to deploy humanoid robots that fulfill up to 30% of client orders, driving operational efficiency and growth.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, Highline's 60K SF footprint and rapid expansion in Sunset Park, combined with adjacent mixed-use (MADE Bush Terminal), signals sustained tech-enabled logistics density in post-industrial Brooklyn. This may compress available industrial land for conversion to residential/mixed-use and sustain high industrial rents, which can support higher ground-floor commercial rents in mixed-use projects. For multifamily developers seeking logistics/industrial ground-floor anchors, Sunset Park's roster (Highline, MADE, Industry City) demonstrates strong operator demand and absorption, suggesting mixed-use multifamily with light-industrial ground floors may perform well in the neighborhood.

stories.title: Bakery Shop Cafe Aroma to Open New Spot on the Upper East Side
stories.excerpt: Time to rise and grind, Upper East Siders, as a new coffee shop and bakery is set to open at Avenues Equity Partners’ 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill. Family-owned coffee shop and bakery Cafe Aroma has taken 2,200 square feet of retail space at the base of the five-story residential building, according to landlord broker Meridian Capital Group. The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for retail space on the Upper East Side was $252 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. Meridian’s Jacob Mayer represented the landlord in this deal, while Shai Baradarian and Tamir Baradarian of eRealty Advisors represented Cafe Aroma. The Baradarians did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “1410 Madison Avenue is a rare turnkey restaurant space in a high-traffic Upper East Side location, and it generated strong interest from operators looking to be near Mount Sinai and Carnegie Hill’s dense residential base,” Mayer said in a statement. “Cafe Aroma is a great fit for the space, and we’re pleased to have completed a lease that positions both the landlord and the tenant for long-term success in the corridor.” This will be Cafe Aroma’s sixth Manhattan location and its first on the Upper East Side. Avenues Equity Partners acquired 1410 Madison Avenue and 1412 Madison Avenue for $6.3 million in January 2025, according to property records. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Cafe Aroma, family-owned coffee/bakery, signed 2,200 SF retail lease at 1410 Madison Avenue (Carnegie Hill, Upper East Side) in five-story residential building.
Upper East Side retail average asking rent Q2 2026: $252/SF; location cited for proximity to Mount Sinai, dense residential base.
Neighborhood retail leasing active; coffee/café tenants filling ground-floor spaces in residential buildings.
stories.summary: Family-owned coffee shop and bakery Cafe Aroma signed a 2,200-square-foot retail lease at 1410 Madison Avenue in the Carnegie Hill neighborhood of Manhattan's Upper East Side. The space is at the base of a five-story residential building owned by Avenues Equity Partners. Meridian Capital Group represented the landlord. Average asking rent for Upper East Side retail was $252 per square foot in Q2 2026. The location is near Mount Sinai and Carnegie Hill's dense residential population.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily development. However, successful ground-floor café leasing in residential buildings on the UES suggests strong retail demand and density-driven foot traffic, which supports value-add or new multifamily projects with retail components. The $252/SF asking rent for neighborhood retail (vs. ~$150–$200/SF in secondary markets) indicates premium pricing power in high-density residential neighborhoods, justifying higher mixed-use development density and ROI in Upper East Side conversions and new construction.

stories.title: Hudson Bay Capital Supplies $60M Refi for Redeveloped N.J. Office Campus
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Somerset Development and HIG Capital has sealed a $60 million loan to refinance a repurposed New Jersey mixed-use office campus that will soon be home to sandwich chain Jersey’s Mike’s headquarters, Commercial Observer has learned. Hudson Bay Capital provided the bridge loan for the sponsorship’s 276,900-square-foot property Bell Works Fort Monmouth property in Tinton Falls, N.J. Commvault, a data protection firm, developed the property as its headquarters in 2014 before selling it to Somerset last year as part of a plan to reimagine the site as flexible office and retail space. “By reimagining an underutilized suburban campus as a vibrant mixed-use destination, we’ve created lasting value for investors, tenants and the community,” Ralph Zucker, president and CEO of Inspired by Somerset Development, said in a statement. “This financing reinforces Bell Works’ role as a catalyst for the continued revitalization of Fort Monmouth.” Located at 1 CommVault Way near where Netflix is building a $1 billion studio complex, Bell Works Fort Monmouth has inked 222,000 square feet of leases since Somerset redeveloped it, the website TAPinto Middletown reported last month. Jersey Mike’s signed a 72,326-square-foot lease to relocate its corporate headquarters there from Manasquan, N.J., and also leased a 2,286 square-foot retail location. The deals bring the property’s occupancy to 88 percent, according to the publication. Commvault, which formerly was the sole tenant of Bell Works, has maintained its headquarters at the campus and occupies 101,878 square feet. Walker & Dunlop negotiated the debt with a capital markets institutional advisory team consisting of Dustin Stolly, Aaron Appel, Sean Reimer, Keith Kurland, Jonathan Schwartz, Adam Schwartz, Ari Hirt and Stanley Cayre. “Bell Works Fort Monmouth is a highly differentiated mixed-use campus that continues to demonstrate strong leasing momentum and long-term value creation,” Stolly, senior
bullets: Hudson Bay Capital provided $60M bridge loan to Somerset Development/HIG Capital JV for Bell Works Fort Monmouth refinance in Tinton Falls, New Jersey.
276.9K SF redeveloped office campus; Jersey Mike's headquarters incoming; mixed-use flexible office/retail repositioning.
Former Commvault HQ (2014); near Netflix $1B studio complex; suburban NJ mixed-use gaining refinance capital.
stories.summary: A joint venture between Somerset Development and HIG Capital closed a $60 million bridge loan from Hudson Bay Capital to refinance Bell Works Fort Monmouth, a 276,900-square-foot mixed-use office campus in Tinton Falls, New Jersey. Originally developed as Commvault's headquarters in 2014, Somerset acquired the property last year and has been repositioning it as flexible office and retail space. Jersey Mike's sandwich chain will soon establish its headquarters at the site. The campus is located near Netflix's $1 billion studio complex under construction in the region.
stories.impact: Suburban NJ mixed-use office-to-flex repositioning gaining refinance capital suggests asset-class demand for adaptive-reuse office campuses outside major metros. While not multifamily, the trend signals capital appetite for mixed-use conversions in suburban corridors with HQ/corporate anchors. For multifamily developers, Jersey Mike's HQ anchor (and proximate Netflix studio) may drive worker housing demand in Tinton Falls/Monmouth County exurbs, creating opportunity for workforce multifamily near Fort Monmouth. However, the $60M refi for 277K SF office/retail suggests low leverage and modest returns, implying market caution on suburban flex office; residential-focused developers should target this corridor early before office-to-residential conversion becomes cost-prohibitive.

stories.title: New Empire Corporation Buys Midtown Parking Garage for $33M
stories.excerpt: Bentley Zhao’s New Empire Corporation, a New York City-based developer of residential mixed-use buildings, dropped $33.5 million to acquire a Midtown parking garage at 10 East 30th Street, according to a deed filed in property records on Thursday. The purchase, made through the entity Rolls Yacht Tower Management, follows an application filed by the developer last week with the New York City Department of Buildings to demolish the seven-story, 48,500-square-foot garage. Zhao has yet to disclose his exact plans for the demolition site, but New Empire’s website advertises the address as part of the company’s “Manhattan expansion.” Roughly four blocks east, at 567 Second Avenue, New Empire is planning a 14-story luxury condominium building, according to the website. A spokesman for New Empire did not respond to a request for comment. The deal was made with a $20 million mortgage from Ponce Bank. It is unclear whether either party in the purchase worked with outside brokerages. The seller, Aero Parking, has operated the parking garage between Madison and Fifth avenues since 1995, according to property records. New Empire recently entered into a contract to acquire another Midtown development, this one a seven-story office property at 4 West 43rd Street. The property was advertised as an office-to-residential redevelopment opportunity. A representative for Aero Parking did not respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: New Empire Corporation (Bentley Zhao) purchased Midtown Manhattan parking garage (10 East 30th Street) for $33.5M; filed demolition permit.
Seven-story, 48.5K SF structure; $20M Ponce Bank mortgage; New Empire plots Manhattan expansion and luxury condo development.
Nearby, New Empire planning 14-story luxury condo at 567 Second Avenue; single-family teardowns continue in prime Manhattan submarkets.
stories.summary: New Empire Corporation, a NYC-based developer of residential mixed-use buildings, purchased a seven-story, 48,500-square-foot parking garage at 10 East 30th Street in Midtown Manhattan for $33.5 million. The developer filed an application to demolish the structure. New Empire financed the purchase with a $20 million mortgage from Ponce Bank. The developer has not disclosed exact plans for the site but has listed the address as part of its "Manhattan expansion" portfolio. Nearby at 567 Second Avenue, New Empire is planning a 14-story luxury condominium building.
stories.impact: Parking garage acquisition and demolition for Manhattan residential redevelopment signals ongoing site assembly by residential developers in constrained Midtown submarkets. New Empire's $33.5M purchase for 48.5K SF parking (≈$690/SF land cost) and adjacent 14-story luxury condo pipeline indicates strong developer confidence in Midtown residential fundamentals despite office headwinds. For multifamily operators, continued parking-to-residential conversions in Midtown reduce operational parking supply and may justify higher residential rents in car-free or reduced-parking units. However, condo development (not rental) limits direct multifamily relevance; the macro signal is that Midtown remains a target for luxury residential capital despite office market softness.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Wit Tech (SpaceX-affiliated company) planning $5.2M renovation at Seaholm Power Plant, Downtown Austin; Musk companies occupy 35K SF at 800 West Cesar Chavez.
Elon Musk pursuing $55B Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County (6,000+ acres); tax breaks and TIF approved; 100M SF potential scope.
Austin office space absorption driven by Musk ecosystem; suburban industrial/manufacturing expansion signals downstream workforce housing demand.
stories.summary: Multiple Elon Musk-affiliated companies are renovating office space at Downtown Austin's Seaholm Power Plant. Wit Tech, a company with SpaceX links, is planning a $5.2 million facelift for 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street occupied by Musk entities. Separately, Musk is developing the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing facility in Grimes County, a $55 billion project on 6,000+ acres near Bastrop, east of Austin. Grimes County Commissioners approved tax breaks and a tax increment reinvestment zone. Musk has hinted the facility could eventually expand to 100 million square feet. The project is advancing despite local environmental and property-value concerns.
stories.impact: Musk's $55B Terafab semiconductor plant and 100M SF potential scope in Grimes County represents a generational job-creation anchor for Central Texas and Austin exurbs. Office absorption (35K SF Downtown) plus manufacturing/semiconductor capex will drive inbound professional and technical payroll. For multifamily developers, this signals urgent opportunity to acquire/entitle land in Bastrop, Grimes, and East Austin corridors within 20–30 miles of Terafab before land prices spike. Expect strong workforce housing demand 2027–2032 as construction ramps and operations begin; developers entering now will capture highest returns. Tax increment financing (TIF) approval also suggests municipal support for infrastructure and subsidized housing—coordinate with local housing authorities to blend multifamily with local incentives.

stories.title: Florida’s largest Live Local Act project is bold. Its ambitious developer is controversial
stories.excerpt: Pablo Castro came into South Florida real estate with a bang. Over the past two years, the Spanish developer, who has never built in the U.S. and until recently had zero name recognition here, proposed Florida’s largest known multifamily complex, bought the site, secured approval and stirred controversy: Castro faces lawsuits by three local industry players who say they were stiffed after playing a role in laying the groundwork for the project.  HueHub would consist of 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River. It is planned under the state’s Live Local Act, which allows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pablo Castro (Spanish developer, new to U.S. market) developed HueHub in West Little River, Miami: 4,040 units across 7 towers under Florida Live Local Act.
All units furnished and attainably priced (exceeds 40% affordable requirement); 100% affordable/workforce housing mix; free amenities (gym, learning center, kitchens).
Castro faces three lawsuits from local industry players alleging non-payment; project approved and site acquired; largest known Live Local multifamily complex.
stories.summary: Pablo Castro, a Spanish multifamily developer new to the U.S. market, is developing HueHub in West Little River, Miami—Florida's largest known multifamily complex under the state's Live Local Act. The project comprises 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres. All units are furnished and attainably priced, exceeding the Live Local requirement of 40% below-market-rate units. The development includes free amenities (kitchens, fitness studios, learning center) and is designed for cost-burdened tenants. Castro has acquired the site and secured approval, but faces three lawsuits from local industry players alleging non-payment for services rendered.
stories.impact: HueHub represents a test case for Florida's Live Local Act as an alternative financing/entitlement pathway for ultra-large affordable/workforce multifamily, bypassing traditional LIHTC/subsidy complexity. The 4,040-unit scale and 100% attainable-pricing model signal new-market entrant confidence in Miami rental fundamentals and permitting speed under Live Local. However, Castro's litigation history raises developer/counterparty risk and may deter institutional co-investment if disputes persist. For multifamily developers in Florida, HueHub's approval demonstrates Live Local viability for large-scale projects; prioritize site control and title clarity before engaging with inexperienced or undercapitalized sponsors. If HueHub delivers successfully, expect a wave of large Live Local submissions in Miami, Tampa, and Jacksonville, accelerating affordable supply and moderating rent growth in those markets.

stories.title: Faena Residences developers presell $30M penthouse for record price along Miami River
stories.excerpt: Shahab Karmely and Edgardo Defortuna’s joint venture presold a penthouse at their Faena-branded luxury condo development along the Miami River for $30 million, a record for the area on price as well as price per square foot.  Karmely’s KAR Properties has long planned to develop the waterfront site into the Rafael Viñoly-designed project. In 2022, Karmely brought on Defortuna’s Fortune International Group as a partner. Last year, they launched the Faena Residences.  A mystery buyer is under contract to buy a penthouse in the east tower that will span 8,200 square feet of interior space and also include 7,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Shahab Karmely (KAR Properties) and Edgardo Defortuna (Fortune International Group) presold penthouse at Faena Residences (Miami River) for $30M on 8,200 SF interior + 7K SF outdoor space.
Duplex penthouse with private rooftop, pool, wellness space; presale price ~$3,700/SF interior; breaks record for Miami River neighborhood.
Record-setting presale before groundbreaking; signals ultra-luxury condo market depth and investor appetite for branded, waterfront Miami luxury.
stories.summary: Faena Residences, a Rafael Viñoly-designed luxury condo development along the Miami River by Shahab Karmely (KAR Properties) and Edgardo Defortuna (Fortune International Group), presold a penthouse in the east tower for $30 million. The duplex unit spans 8,200 square feet of interior space plus 7,000 square feet of outdoor space (private rooftop with pool, wellness, entertainment). The presale breaks down to approximately $3,700 per square foot and sets a new neighborhood record. The presale occurred before project groundbreaking, reflecting strong investor demand for branded luxury waterfront residential in Miami.
stories.impact: Record-setting $30M penthouse presale (before groundbreaking) on Miami River indicates ultra-luxury condo market remains robust despite broader multifamily headwinds, driven by wealth preservation and waterfront scarcity. However, this is condo, not multifamily rental, limiting direct relevance. The presale success may pull capital away from multifamily rental development in Miami as investors prioritize higher-ASP condo projects with faster presale absorption. For rental developers, the ultra-luxury condo strength at $3,700/SF suggests limited downside risk to premium rentals at $3.00–$4.00/SF in similar Miami River/Brickell locations, but expect slower lease-up if condos continue to absorb top-tier renters seeking ownership and lifestyle branding.

stories.title: LA city’s top construction permits: Week ending July 11, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 The largest development to receive permits last week was for a single-family build in Encino, where plans were filed for 11,278 square feet of work across two permits at 3864 North Kim Lane. The project includes a new two-story single-family dwelling with an attached three-car garage and a pond. Job Filing Numbers: 25010-10000-05697, 25010-10000-05874, 25047-10000-01751 and more 📊 In Valley Glen, permits were issued for 8,765 square feet of construction across three permits at 14414-14418 1/2 West Tiara Street. The project includes building a new accessory dwelling unit, along with a three-story duplex. The work is estimated to cost […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LA city permits week ending July 11, 2026: Encino single-family (11.3K SF), Valley Glen duplex + ADU (8.8K SF, $880K cost), Bel Air megamansion 'The One' alterations (75.9K SF, $350K+).
Largest demolition: Studio City project (details truncated); Van Nuys Golf Club alterations (16.3K SF driving range, $1.1M).
Single-family and luxury residential permits dominate LA permitting; accessory dwelling unit supply increasing.
stories.summary: Los Angeles city permits for the week ending July 11, 2026, were dominated by single-family and luxury residential projects. The largest new-build permit was an 11,278-square-foot two-story single-family dwelling with three-car garage and pond in Encino. In Valley Glen, a 8,765-square-foot mixed project (ADU plus three-story duplex) received permits with an estimated cost of $880,500. In Bel Air, the megamansion "The One" (purchased by Fashion Nova owner Richard Saghian in 2022 for $141 million) received 75,856 square feet of alteration permits across 12 permits estimated at over $350,000. Van Nuys Golf Club received permits for 16,333 square feet of driving-range canopy and equipment-room additions ($1.1 million).
stories.impact: LA permitting mix reveals continued single-family and luxury residential construction vigor, along with rising ADU supply (regulatory trend in California metros). Minimal commercial/multifamily density development visible in week snapshot. For multifamily developers, LA's permit composition signals ongoing preference for single-family/ADU infill over rental apartments, consistent with California's residential-zoning restrictiveness. However, ADU uptick may indicate zoning flexibility and renter demand in constrained neighborhoods; developers should target ADU-zoned areas for ground-up multifamily or conversion projects. No major multifamily/mixed-use permits evident; expect multifamily development to remain capital-constrained in LA until office/retail conversion economics improve or zoning reform accelerates.
